subtopics.subtopic_description: Educational background or training
notes: Holds a Doctorate (PhD) in Electrical and Computer Engineering.
PhD research focus on cyber-physical systems and large language models.
Completed undergraduate studies in a related engineering field prior to PhD.
Has received formal training in machine learning concepts during doctoral studies.
Participated in research workshops and academic conferences related to AI and robotics.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific job title and role description
notes: Currently a first-year PhD researcher in Electrical and Computer Engineering.
Works as a Mechatronics Engineer with a research focus.
Role involves conducting academic research and hands-on experiments in AI-driven design automation.
Responsible for developing and fine-tuning AI models for engineering applications.
Regularly collaborates with academic advisors and research peers.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Current industry or sector (e.g., tech, finance, manufacturing)
notes: Works in the academic research sector, specifically within engineering.
Operates at the intersection of AI technology and mechatronics engineering.
Focuses on applications relevant to the tech and manufacturing industries.
Engages in cross-disciplinary work combining computer science and engineering.
Research aligns with industry trends in automation and intelligent design systems.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Company size and environment
notes: Affiliated with a large research university or academic institution.
Works within a small research group or lab (5-15 members).
Collaborates with both faculty and fellow graduate students.
Environment characterized by academic rigor and a culture of innovation.
Access to shared university resources, labs, and computational clusters.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Type of business or market segment
notes: Academic research with a focus on advanced engineering solutions.
Market segment includes AI-enabled design automation and robotics.
Research outputs have potential applications in manufacturing automation.
Engages with both the academic and industrial research communities.
Work is often published in academic journals and presented at conferences.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Duration/years of experience in current role
notes: Has less than 1 year of experience in the current research role.
Started PhD program and research position at age 23.
Prior research internships or assistantships during undergraduate studies.
Currently in the first year of doctoral research.
Rapidly building expertise in AI-driven engineering within the past year.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Professional seniority or career level
notes: Early-career researcher and graduate-level engineer.
Holds the position of PhD student, equivalent to junior-level in industry.
Primarily responsible for research execution rather than project leadership.
Works under supervision and mentorship of faculty advisors.
Career trajectory aimed at progressing to postdoctoral or industry research roles.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Primary job responsibilities and regular daily tasks
notes: Fine-tunes large language models (e.g., Llama 3.2) for engineering use cases.
Prepares and curates datasets for model training and evaluation.
Develops code frameworks and scripts for model integration into CAD workflows.
Conducts literature reviews and paper searches to inform research design.
Collaborates with peers and advisors to discuss research progress and troubleshooting.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Approximate proportion of time spent on core activities
notes: Spends approximately 40% of time on AI model fine-tuning and coding tasks.
Allocates about 25% of time to literature review and information gathering.
Dedicates 20% of time to dataset preparation and preprocessing.
Reserves 10% for meetings with advisors and research discussions.
Remaining 5% spent on documentation and result analysis.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of autonomy and scope of decision-making in the role
notes: Has moderate autonomy in choosing research methods and tools.
Can independently design and execute model fine-tuning experiments.
Major research directions are set in consultation with academic advisors.
Responsible for day-to-day task prioritization and workflow management.
Final decisions on publications or major pivots require advisor approval.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that feel easiest or most natural to perform
notes: Using ChatGPT or Copilot for code generation and debugging.
Literature search and summarization of research papers using LLMs.
Basic data preprocessing and dataset organization tasks.
Experimenting with different model hyperparameters.
Utilizing VS Code and related development tools for script editing.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks perceived as most challenging or complex
notes: Designing effective datasets for fine-tuning LLMs on novel engineering tasks.
Diagnosing and resolving model performance issues specific to CAD file generation.
Integrating AI-generated outputs seamlessly into engineering workflows.
Assessing the feasibility and robustness of innovative research ideas.
Understanding and applying advanced concepts outside original field of study (e.g., deep learning theory).

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are repetitive, data-heavy, or suitable for automation
notes: Preprocessing large datasets for model training.
Running multiple fine-tuning experiments with minor parameter variations.
Extracting and formatting data from academic literature databases.
Automated generation and validation of code snippets.
Routine documentation updates and result logging.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are most enjoyable or engaging versus those that feel boring or tedious
notes: Finds research ideation and creative problem-solving highly engaging.
Enjoys rapid prototyping and testing new AI-based approaches.
Derives satisfaction from successful integration of LLMs into engineering workflows.
Considers repetitive data cleaning and administrative reporting tedious.
Routine literature review can feel monotonous without clear research direction.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Common pain points or inefficiencies in completing tasks
notes: Difficulty in sourcing high-quality, domain-specific datasets for fine-tuning.
Time-consuming manual evaluation of AI model outputs for technical accuracy.
Interoperability issues between AI tools and legacy engineering software.
Redundant steps in data annotation and preprocessing tasks.
Lack of automated quality checks for generated CAD files.

subtopics.subtopic_description: How enjoyment, skill level, and productivity relate to one another
notes: Higher enjoyment is often linked to tasks where AI tools increase productivity.
Skill level rapidly increases when working on engaging, hands-on coding tasks.
Productivity drops during repetitive or unclear research phases.
Learning new AI techniques can be both challenging and rewarding, driving skill growth.
Enjoyment is highest when seeing direct impact of work on research outcomes.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Attitude towards learning new technologies and tools
notes: Highly motivated to learn new AI technologies relevant to research.
Views technology adoption as essential for staying current in the field.
Enjoys experimenting with emerging LLMs and automation tools.
Sees learning new tools as a path to increased research efficiency.
Rarely feels intimidated by unfamiliar software.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived adaptability to new software/methods
notes: Adapts quickly to new coding environments and AI frameworks.
Regularly updates workflows to incorporate the latest LLM advancements.
Comfortable switching between different programming languages as needed.
Able to self-learn through online resources and documentation.
Often among the earliest in peer group to try new AI tools.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness to invest time in tech training
notes: Willing to dedicate significant time to mastering new AI-related tools.
Attends workshops and online tutorials outside formal coursework.
Prioritizes hands-on experimentation over passive learning.
Considers tech training a necessary investment for career growth.
Balances tech learning with research deadlines and deliverables.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Motivations or barriers to learning new tech (e.g., workload, relevance)
notes: Strongly motivated by the direct relevance to research productivity.
Perceives high workload and tight project timelines as occasional barriers.
Less inclined to learn tools with limited applicability to current projects.
Peer recommendations can positively influence tool adoption.
Occasional frustration when documentation is lacking or unclear.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Influence of peers or management on willingness to adopt new tools
notes: Peers often share tips and discoveries about new AI tools.
Advisors encourage exploration of novel technologies for research.
Collaborative lab culture fosters rapid adoption of innovative software.
Participates in group discussions about tool effectiveness.
Positive feedback from peers accelerates willingness to invest in learning.

topic: Primary Tools and Technologies Used in Work

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific software, platforms, or systems used daily
notes: Uses VS Code as primary code editor for all script development.
Employs GitHub Copilot for automated code generation and suggestions.
Frequently interacts with ChatGPT for research and coding assistance.
Leverages Python and relevant ML libraries (e.g., PyTorch, TensorFlow) for model work.
Uses Chrome and other browsers for search and literature review.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Essential non-AI tools for workflow
notes: Relies on version control systems like Git for project management.
Utilizes LaTeX editors for research paper writing and documentation.
Employs data annotation tools for dataset preparation.
Uses spreadsheet software (Excel/Google Sheets) for data analysis and tracking.
CAD software is sometimes used for manual file inspection and validation.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with industry-standard technologies
notes: Familiar with leading LLMs such as Llama 3.2 and OpenAI GPT models.
Experience with Python-based machine learning frameworks.
Knows basics of CAD software used in engineering design.
Understands principles of code versioning and collaborative development.
Aware of cloud-based computing resources for large-scale model training.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Interoperability or integration issues between tools
notes: Sometimes encounters incompatibility between AI model outputs and CAD software formats.
Manual conversion steps are occasionally required between data annotation and model processing tools.
Integrating code from Copilot suggestions needs careful validation for engineering standards.
Workflow bottlenecks occur when tools lack direct APIs for automation.
Occasional difficulties in synchronizing data across different software platforms.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with fundamental AI/ML concepts and terminology
notes: Comfortable discussing concepts such as fine-tuning, transfer learning, and model evaluation.
Understands the basics of neural network architectures and training dynamics.
Knows standard metrics for assessing AI model performance.
Familiar with terminology around generative AI and language models.
Keeps up to date with latest AI research publications.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Names of specific AI software/platforms currently used in work
notes: Uses Llama 3.2 model for engineering-specific generative tasks.
Employs ChatGPT for code generation and research assistance.
Utilizes GitHub Copilot for auto-completion and code suggestions.
Occasionally runs experiments with other open-source LLMs (e.g., Falcon, Mistral).
Explores Hugging Face platform for model repositories and utilities.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Frequency and purpose of AI tool application (specific use cases)
notes: Applies AI tools daily for code framework generation and debugging.
Uses LLMs for automated generation of CAD files from engineering prompts.
Relies on conversational AI for literature review and idea validation.
Employs Copilot for accelerating repetitive coding tasks.
Integrates AI into research pipeline for proof-of-concept demonstrations.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific examples of AI success and failure experiences (lessons learned)
notes: Successfully fine-tuned Llama 3.2 to produce valid CAD file templates.
AI-assisted literature review led to faster identification of key research gaps.
Encountered model hallucinations when domain-specific data was insufficient.
Recognized the need for manual validation of AI-generated engineering outputs.
Learned to iterate on dataset quality to improve model reliability.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of organizational training or peer resources for AI use
notes: Receives informal guidance from research advisors on AI best practices.
Peers in the lab often share AI tool tips and troubleshooting advice.
Occasionally attends university-hosted AI workshops and seminars.
No formal company-wide AI training program; learning is largely self-driven.
Access to online courses and documentation is encouraged by advisors.

topic: AI Interaction Style and Workflow Change

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preferred mode of interaction: independent versus step-by-step collaboration
notes: Prefers an iterative, step-by-step collaboration with AI tools.
Frequently seeks feedback from LLMs during research ideation.
Enjoys refining AI-generated outputs based on personal expertise.
Uses AI both independently and as a collaborative assistant.
Combines initial AI suggestions with manual adjustments.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Style of human-AI teaming (e.g., advisor, assistant, co-worker)
notes: Views AI as a knowledgeable assistant for research and coding.
Occasionally treats AI as a brainstorming partner for new ideas.
Relies on AI as an advisor for feasibility assessments.
Maintains final decision-making authority on research outputs.
Uses AI to augment, not replace, core engineering expertise.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness and openness to adopting new AI-driven workflows
notes: Highly open to integrating new AI-driven workflows when beneficial.
Regularly experiments with updated LLM capabilities in research pipeline.
Encourages lab peers to adopt AI-augmented processes.
Willing to adjust established habits if AI tools improve efficiency.
Evaluates new workflows for both productivity and reliability.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preference for conversational vs. command-based interfaces (communication dynamics)
notes: Prefers conversational interfaces like ChatGPT for brainstorming and Q&A.
Finds natural language prompts more intuitive than rigid command syntax.
Occasionally uses command-line tools for batch processing and automation.
Appreciates AI systems that can adapt to both conversational and technical input.
Believes conversational UIs lower barriers for non-CS researchers.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Extent to which tasks rely on specialized, tacit domain knowledge
notes: Engineering research tasks often require nuanced domain understanding.
Fine-tuning LLMs for CAD output demands technical expertise in both AI and engineering.
Manual review is necessary to ensure AI-generated outputs meet project requirements.
Tacit knowledge is critical when evaluating the feasibility of novel research ideas.
AI assistance bridges knowledge gaps but cannot fully replace domain expertise.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of trust in AI outputs for work tasks and critical decisions
notes: Trusts AI-generated code for boilerplate and standard tasks, with manual review for critical sections.
Moderate trust in AI-generated CAD files, always followed by validation.
High trust in AI for literature aggregation, but not for final research conclusions.
Skeptical of AI outputs when applied to highly novel or complex engineering cases.
Prefers to verify all AI-generated artifacts before integration into core research.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Ideal balance of human effort and AI automation for specific tasks
notes: Prefers AI automation for repetitive and time-consuming coding tasks.
Human expertise prioritized for final evaluation and creative ideation.
Seeks a workflow where AI accelerates routine work, freeing time for innovation.
Advocates for a hybrid approach: AI for draft generation, human for refinement.
Believes fully automated pipelines are only viable for low-risk tasks.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Conditions under which high automation is acceptable or threatening
notes: Accepts high automation for well-defined, low-stakes engineering tasks.
Automation is threatening when model outputs lack transparency or explainability.
Prefers manual oversight for tasks with safety or academic integrity implications.
Worries about over-reliance on AI for novel research directions.
Considers automation beneficial when it augments, not replaces, human judgment.

topic: AI Impact on Skills and Job Security

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived impact of AI on the importance of existing skills (enhanced vs. reduced)
notes: AI tools enhance the value of interdisciplinary skills (engineering + AI).
Routine coding skills become less critical as AI automation increases.
Critical thinking and domain expertise are now more important than before.
AI lowers barriers to entry for non-CS backgrounds, but deep expertise remains essential.
Manual technical skills are still needed for error-checking and troubleshooting.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Emerging skills or new areas of responsibility created by AI
notes: Data curation and prompt engineering have become essential new skills.
Responsible for evaluating and correcting AI-generated outputs.
Needs to stay current with rapid advancements in AI model capabilities.
Increased focus on interdisciplinary collaboration between AI and engineering domains.
Documentation of AI workflows and results is a growing responsibility.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of concern about AI replacing specific tasks or the overall role
notes: Mild concern that AI could replace repetitive or basic coding tasks.
Confident that creative research and problem-solving cannot be fully automated.
Believes core engineering roles are evolving, not disappearing, due to AI.
Sees AI as an opportunity to focus on higher-level, value-added work.
Remains attentive to ethical implications of AI-driven job shifts.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of override mechanisms or manual checks for AI-driven processes
notes: Manual review is standard for all AI-generated code and CAD files.
Research workflow includes checkpoints for human validation after AI output.
No fully automated deployment of AI outputs without human sign-off.
Lab policy requires documentation of any significant AI-driven changes.
Override mechanisms are built into code pipelines to halt or revise AI results.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived change in team or company policies regarding AI adoption
notes: Research lab increasingly encourages use of AI tools in project workflows.
Advisors advocate for responsible, transparent use of AI technologies.
No formal policies restricting AI use, but ethical guidelines are discussed.
Peers regularly share experiences and best practices for AI adoption.
Expectations for AI literacy among lab members have risen over the past year.

subtopics.subtopic_description: General outlook on AI's broader societal and industry impact
notes: Believes AI will significantly accelerate innovation in engineering and manufacturing.
Sees AI as a tool to democratize access to complex technical fields.
Acknowledges potential for AI to disrupt traditional job roles.
Optimistic about AI's role in improving research productivity and creativity.
Cautious about unintended consequences, such as over-reliance on automation.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Personal beliefs about the ethics and risks of AI in the workplace
notes: Views transparency and explainability as essential for ethical AI use.
Concerned about risks of bias or hallucination in AI-generated outputs.
Believes human oversight should remain central in critical decision-making.
Supports development of clear ethical guidelines for AI-driven research.
Advocates for ongoing education about potential risks and limitations.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Missing AI tools or features that would be most beneficial in the future
notes: Desires AI tools with better integration into engineering-specific software.
Wishes for more explainable AI outputs tailored to technical audiences.
Needs automated quality assurance for AI-generated CAD files.
Would benefit from more robust dataset generation and validation tools.
Hopes for collaborative AI agents that can work across multiple research tasks.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Predicted evolution of their job in the next 5-10 years with AI integration
notes: Expects increased reliance on AI for routine engineering design and testing.
Foresees a shift towards higher-level research and creative problem-solving.
Anticipates need for advanced skills in managing and interpreting AI outputs.
Believes interdisciplinary teams will become standard in engineering research.
Predicts new research opportunities at the intersection of AI and physical systems.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Concrete steps they would want their organization to take regarding AI strategy
notes: Implement structured training on responsible AI use for all researchers.
Develop clear guidelines for validation and documentation of AI-driven results.
Invest in infrastructure to support seamless integration of AI into engineering workflows.
Encourage interdisciplinary collaboration between AI specialists and domain experts.
Foster a culture of continuous learning and ethical awareness around AI adoption.
